mappings.vsiAuthor: Steven W. Lockley, Russell G. Foster
mappings.rationale: Directly covers the state of sleep and its effects on health, including REM, NREM, light and deep sleep, and dreaming (section D.3).
Directly covers nutrition and diet including functions of food, classes of food, recommended nutrient intakes, and therapeutic diets (section D.1).
Directly covers the immune response and defenses against disease, including inflammation and phagocytic cells (section C.2).
Directly covers aging as a stage in the human life cycle (section A.6) and the health implications of the aging process.
Relevant to the alarm reaction and hormonal preparation for flight or resistance (section C.5), and endocrine regulation of health maintenance.
Covers the role of blood in prevention of hemorrhage (section C.3) and blood's functions in maintaining bodily health.
Covers prenatal development, birth, and postnatal development (sections A.2, A.3, A.4) as stages in the human life cycle.
Covers the physiological mechanisms for maintaining health, internal environment stability, and bodily responses to stress.
Covers food science relevant to nutrition, feeding behaviour, and the functions and classes of food (section D.1).
Biological rhythms are fundamental to health maintenance, affecting sleep cycles, hormone regulation, and the body's internal environment.
Covers a key stage in the human life cycle between postnatal development and reproduction (sections A.4 and A.5).
The human microbiome plays critical roles in maintaining health, immune defense, and nutrition absorption.
Relevant to understanding cellular adaptation to severe stress (section C.1) and the processes of regeneration and tissue repair (section C.4).
Relevant to healing processes, regeneration and organization in the repair of tissues (section C.4).
Relevant to exercise and physical conditioning, including health maintenance and physiological responses to exercise (section D.2).
Directly covers dreaming as part of the psychophysiological variations in sleep (section D.3.b).
Addresses the human body in relation to health, fitness, and the social and biological dimensions of bodily well-being.
Relevant to understanding genetic factors in health, including congenital conditions affecting the human life cycle from fertilization onward.
